normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-PLATFORM-2 (Description): WebViews are typically used by apps that have a need for increased control over the UI. This control ensures that WebViews are configured securely to prevent sensitive data leakage as well as sensitive functionality exposure (e.g. via JavaScript bridges to native code).
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps that embed WebViews must configure them to prevent sensitive data leakage and sensitive functionality exposure. WebView cookies and cache must clear on dismissal and session end; JavaScript bridges to native code must be scoped to declared origins only; arbitrary URL loading must be restricted; file:// and content:// scheme access must be disabled unless explicitly required; and JavaScript execution must be disabled where not needed.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note:
Applies to every Tier 2+ app that embeds WebViews. Per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime), WebView discipline activates at Tier 2+ where the app handles content distribution and analytics. The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete WebView-usage boolean at the technical layer because security_manifest.webview_discipline is the per-app declaration block (present at Tier 2+ per the existing schema); apps that don't embed WebViews declare an empty/no-webview posture there. The tier-floor applicability is the honest proxy until the schema gains a precise webview-presence field. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 2+ that embeds WebViews uses WKWebView on iOS or WebView on Android because in-app browsers, OAuth flows, and licensed-content rendering are baseline Tier 2+ surfaces.

**Phase 5 migration (2026-05-21):** Applicability condition migrated to a direct-field check on the per-surface evidence array that backs this control. The new condition asks whether the app actually declares the surface this control governs, replacing the prior tier-or-auth-flow proxy. tier_floor stays unchanged as the universal-floor backstop. References Phase 1 schema addition (commit `9ad9b6c`), Phase 4 applicability evaluator (commit `37ce03d`), and Phase 4.5 evaluator `==` operator extension (Phase 5 commit).

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5's SEC-PILLAR-5-WEBVIEW-DISCIPLINE trace and MASVS-PLATFORM-2 describe the same control: WebViews configured securely. Both require cookies and cache cleared on dismissal, JavaScript bridges scoped to declared origins, restricted URL loading, and protection against sensitive data leakage through WebView state. The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control are co-extensive — WebView discipline is WebView discipline. Implementation correctness for per-platform settings (WKWebViewConfiguration on iOS, WebSettings on Android), JavaScript bridge scoping (WKScriptMessageHandler origin checks on iOS, addJavascriptInterface origin restrictions on Android), and the no-file-scheme rule requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is direct at the control-statement level, but the auditor evaluation chain (file_standards_auditor + reaper-mobile + reaper-input + reaper-secrets) is necessary for full evaluation of correctness.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: WKWebView with WKWebViewConfiguration declaring restricted preferences — javaScriptEnabled set per declared need (false where not required), allowsContentJavaScript declared in WKWebpagePreferences
iOS: WKUserContentController message handlers scoped to specific name allowlist; each handler validates source frame origin against declared allowlist before invoking native code
iOS: WKHTTPCookieStore cleared on dismissal via removeAllCookiesFromDataStore or equivalent for sensitive sessions

Android: WebView with WebSettings.setJavaScriptEnabled() set per declared need; setAllowFileAccess(false) and setAllowFileAccessFromFileURLs(false) and setAllowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs(false) for production
Android: addJavascriptInterface bridges scoped to declared origins via WebViewClient.shouldOverrideUrlLoading allowlist check before exposing the interface to a loaded URL
Android: CookieManager.getInstance().removeAllCookies() called on session end; WebStorage.getInstance().deleteAllData() for HTML5 storage cleanup
security_manifest.webview_discipline declares: cookie-clear-on-dismiss policy, JS-bridge origin allowlist, sensitive-content surfaces, URL-load allowlist or denylist
failure_indicators: iOS: WKWebView with javaScriptEnabled = true loading arbitrary URLs from user input without origin validation — vulnerable to JavaScript injection from compromised pages
iOS: WKScriptMessageHandler that processes message body without validating message.frameInfo.securityOrigin against an allowlist — any loaded URL can invoke native code
Android: addJavascriptInterface exposed before WebViewClient.shouldOverrideUrlLoading enforces origin restrictions — race condition where attacker-controlled URL loads first and gets bridge access

Android: WebSettings.setJavaScriptEnabled(true) combined with setAllowFileAccessFromFileURLs(true) or setAllowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs(true) — universal file:// scheme bypass enables local file exfiltration
Android: WebView loading user-supplied URLs without scheme validation (file://, content://, javascript: schemes accepted) — local resource access + script injection vectors
WebView cookies persisted across sensitive-session boundaries without explicit clearance — session-fixation and token-replay vectors after logout
JavaScript bridge methods that accept untrusted strings and concatenate them into native operations (file paths, SQL, shell commands) — injection vectors at the native boundary
WebView with mixed-content allowed (HTTPS pages loading HTTP subresources) for sensitive flows — MITM exposure of script content
false_positive_traps: WebView handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately load test fixtures via file:// or stub script handlers; these are not production violations.

javaScriptEnabled = true is NOT inherently a violation — WebViews that legitimately render JavaScript-driven content need JS enabled. The violation surface is JS enabled WITHOUT origin allowlist or message-handler origin validation.

WebViews used for static help/about-page rendering (no JavaScript needed, content bundled with app) with javaScriptEnabled = false and no JS bridges are minimal-risk surfaces — security_manifest can declare the static-render posture; full WebView-discipline applies to interactive WebViews only.

OAuth flows that intentionally use WebView-style in-app browsers (where ASWebAuthenticationSession / CustomTabsClient is not appropriate, e.g., enterprise SSO with custom UI) are NOT violations if combined with strict origin allowlists, ephemeral cookie storage, and bridge scoping — the violation surface is unguarded WebView OAuth, not the use of WebView for OAuth.

Persistent cookies across non-sensitive sessions (e.g., user preferences for a content-feed WebView) are NOT violations as long as security_manifest declares which surfaces are non-sensitive and which require clearance — the violation surface is cookies persisting across sensitive-session boundaries without declared scoping.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 WebView discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-WEBVIEW-DISCIPLINE) as primary evidence toward MASVS-PLATFORM-2 coverage
declares WebView configuration posture in security_manifest.webview_discipline (cookie-clear-on-dismiss, JS-bridge origin allowlist, URL-load policy) per MASVS-PLATFORM-2 requirement
scopes JavaScript bridges to declared origins via WKScriptMessageHandler frame-origin checks (iOS) or WebViewClient.shouldOverrideUrlLoading allowlist (Android)
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-PLATFORM-2. MASVS-PLATFORM-2 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-WEBVIEW-DISCIPLINE (primary, direct). The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement. Claims about WebView security depend on which configurations are in scope — claims about JavaScript-bridge isolation require origin-checked message handlers; claims about session-state isolation require cookie/cache clearance on dismissal; claims about scheme restriction require explicit denial of file://, content://, and javascript: scheme loads. Out of scope: third-party WebView libraries that ship their own WebView wrapper (e.g., a vendor SDK's embedded browser) — those carry their own MASVS-PLATFORM-2 obligations under the vendor's threat model and must be evaluated against this control separately. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
